title: Code-Switching Patterns
explanation: Lebanese Arabic naturally mixes with French and English — a legacy of Lebanon's multilingual culture. This is not slang or error; it is standard Lebanese speech. Educated speakers regularly switch between languages mid-sentence. Understanding these patterns is essential for real-world comprehension.

rows: I want to book an appointment

examples.breakdown: Arabic sentence structure with English noun 'shopping' inserted naturally

examples.arabic: هيدا très bien، ما بدي غيرو
examples.transliteration: hayda très bien, mā baddi ghayro
examples.english: This is very good, I don't want anything else
examples.breakdown: Arabic demonstrative + French 'très bien' + Arabic continuation

examples.english: Alright bye, I'll see you tomorrow
examples.breakdown: Arabic يلا + English 'bye' + Arabic closing — typical Lebanese goodbye

title: Idiomatic Verb + Preposition Combos
explanation: Like English phrasal verbs, Lebanese verbs change meaning depending on the preposition that follows. The same verb root combined with different prepositions can produce completely different meanings. Memorizing these as fixed units is more effective than trying to derive the meaning from parts.

rows: to get up / to start doing

examples.arabic: طلع عالآخر إنو ما كان صحيح
examples.transliteration: ṭiliʕ ʕal-ēkhir inno mā kēn ṣaḥīḥ
examples.english: It turned out in the end that it wasn't true
examples.breakdown: طلع عـ (turned out) + الآخر (the end) + إنو (that)

examples.english: Don't get involved in something that's not your business
examples.breakdown: ما تفوت بـ (don't enter into) + موضوع (matter) + مش إلك (not yours)

examples.english: He went back on his word after two days
examples.breakdown: رجع عـ (went back on) + كلامو (his word) + بعد يومين (after two days)

examples.english: He got up in the morning and went to work
examples.breakdown: قام عـ (got up) + الصبح (morning) — here عـ marks time

title: Register Shifting — Formal vs. Casual
explanation: Lebanese speakers shift between formal (MSA-influenced) and casual registers depending on context. Formal register appears in news, speeches, and respectful interactions with elders or officials. Casual register dominates among friends and family. Mastering both — and knowing when to switch — is the mark of true fluency.

examples.english: Excuse me, may I ask you a question? (formal/polite)
examples.breakdown: عفواً (excuse me, formal) + بقدر (can I) + أسألك (ask you) — polite register for strangers
هلا (hey, very casual) + شو الأخبار (what's the news) — friend register

examples.english: Would you like coffee or tea? (respectful)
examples.breakdown: حضرتك (your presence — respectful 'you') — used with elders and officials

examples.english: Want something or aren't you hungry? (casual)
examples.breakdown: بدك (you want, casual) + مش (not) — friend/family register, no formalities
